perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (with certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within thirty (30) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (with certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within ninety (90) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return/destruction period to 90 days might conflict with data privacy regulations that mandate shorter periods for data retention and disposal under certain circumstances, especially concerning Personal Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts 201 CMR 17.00, Standards for The Protection of Personal Information of Residents of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.04(2)(a) states that every person that owns or licenses personal information about a resident of the commonwealth shall develop, implement, maintain, and monitor a comprehensive information security program. Extending the data retention period to 90 days, may violate the data minimization principle embedded within the regulation, which mandates that personal information be retained only as long as necessary for legitimate business purposes and applicable law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the data return/destruction period from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation states this may violate the data minimization principle within 201 CMR 17.04(2)(a), which mandates that personal information be retained only as long as necessary. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards for safeguarding personal information, suggesting that extending the retention period could contradict these standards, creating an inconsistency regarding time-sensitive obligations as it might not align with what is deemed 'necessary' under the law. This creates a potential violation, as the 90-day period might be longer than what is legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall notify National Grid promptly, and in no event later than five (5) days after discovery, in writing, of any unauthorized access, possession, use, destruction or disclosure of Personal Information (a “ Security Breach ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor shall notify National Grid promptly, and in no event later than fifteen (15) days after discovery, in writing, of any unauthorized access, possession, use, destruction or disclosure of Personal Information (a “ Security Breach ”).
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the security breach notification to 15 days conflicts with data breach notification laws that require prompt notification to affected parties and regulatory bodies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rhode Island Identity Theft Protection Act, RIGL § 11-49.3-3
perturbation.law_explanation: RIGL § 11-49.3-3 mandates that any person that maintains or possesses data containing personal information shall provide notice of any breach of the security of the system following discovery without unreasonable delay. A delay of 15 days would likely be viewed as unreasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: General Laws of Rhode Island Section 11-49.3-3. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Rhode Island General Laws Title 11 - Criminal Offenses Chapter 11-49.3 - Identity Theft Protection Act of 2015 Section 11-49.3-3. - Definitions. Universal Citation: RI Gen L § 11-49.3-3. (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 11-49.3-3. Definitions. (a) The following definitions apply to this section: (1) “Breach of the security of the system” means unauthorized access or acquisition of unencrypted, computerized data information that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information maintained by the municipal agency, state agency, or person. Good-faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of the agency for the purposes of the agency is not a breach of the security of the system; provided, that the personal information is not used or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. (2) “Encrypted” means the transformation of data through the use of a one hundred twenty-eight (128) bit or higher algorithmic process into a form in which there is a low probability of assigning meaning without use of a confidential process or key. Data shall not be considered to be encrypted if it is acquired in combination with any key, security code, or password that would permit access to the encrypted data. (3) “Health insurance information” means an individual’s health insurance policy number, subscriber identification number, or any unique identifier used by a health insurer to identify the individual. (4) “Medical information” means any information regarding an individual’s medical history, mental or physical condition, or
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the notification period for a security breach from 5 to 15 days. The Law Explanation states that RIGL § 11-49.3-3 mandates notification of a security breach 'without unreasonable delay' and that a delay of 15 days would likely be viewed as unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Breach of the security of the system' and this is related to Section 11-49.3-3. The change directly contradicts the requirement for notification 'without unreasonable delay,' making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of the Non- Disclosure Agreement and for a period of seven (7) years thereafter, Contractor shall maintain, and provide for National Grid’s review, at National Grid’s request, (a) Contractor’s WISP; and (b) other applicable security program documents, including summaries of its incident response policies, encryption standards and/or other computer security protection policies or procedures, that constitute compliance with applicable Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of the Non- Disclosure Agreement and for a period of two (2) years thereafter, Contractor shall maintain, and provide for National Grid’s review, at National Grid’s request, (a) Contractor’s WISP; and (b) other applicable security program documents, including summaries of its incident response policies, encryption standards and/or other computer security protection policies or procedures, that constitute compliance with applicable Laws.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the maintenance period of WISP documentation to 2 years after the NDA term may violate record-keeping requirements in data security laws which often have longer retention periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.185(c) discusses regulations to further the purposes of the CCPA. Although it does not explicitly mandate a 7-year retention, the obligation to comply with regulations relating to consumer requests implies a need for retaining relevant security program documentation for longer than 2 years post-agreement.

Regulations (a) On or before July 1, 2020, the Attorney General shall solicit broad public participation and adopt regulations to further the purposes of this title, including, but not limited to, the following areas: (1) Updating or adding categories of personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (c) of Section 1798.130 and subdivision (v) of Section 1798.140, and updating or adding categories of sensitive personal information to those enumerated in subdivision (ae) of Section 1798.140 in order to address changes in technology, data collection practices, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns. (2) Updating as needed the definitions of “deidentified” and “unique identifier” to address changes in technology, data collection, obstacles to implementation, and privacy concerns, and adding, modifying, or deleting categories to the definition of designated methods for submitting requests to facilit
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the document maintenance period from seven to two years, potentially violating record-keeping requirements as it relates to consumer requests according to the LLM's explanation of Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.185(c). Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that the CCPA gives consumers the right to know what personal information businesses collect about them. The reduction in the maintenance period directly impacts the consumer's ability to exercise that right, creating a contradiction as the shortened period may not align with the time needed to respond to and fulfill consumer requests under the CCPA. The original contract aligns more closely with data security law, while the change introduces risk of non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of all agreements between the Parties relevant to and in connection with the Purpose, Contractor shall return to National Grid all Personal Information or destroy such Personal Information beyond recovery and certify such destruction in writing to National Grid.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of all agreements between the Parties relevant to and in connection with the Purpose, Contractor may retain all Personal Information indefinitely, and is not required to destroy it, but must continue to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of personal information directly contradicts data minimization principles found in privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR states that personal data shall be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Indefinite retention violates this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of data minimization as outlined in Article 5 of the GDPR. The original text requires the contractor to return or destroy personal information upon termination, aligning with data minimization. The changed text allows the contractor to retain personal information indefinitely, which is a clear violation of the principle that personal data should not be kept longer than necessary for the purposes for which it was processed. The scraped snippet 2 states that personal data should be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed'. Allowing indefinite retention directly opposes this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Where applicable, if, and only with National Grid’s prior consent, Contractor processes Personal Information received from National Grid from the “European Economic Area” or “EEA” (as defined below) in a jurisdiction that is not an approved jurisdiction under the EEA, Contractor shall ensure that it has a legally approved mechanism in place to allow for the international data transfer prior to the transfer of any such Personal Information and Contractor will abide by the obligations under Regulation (EU) 2016/679, the General Data Protection Regulation, fair and lawful use requirements, together with any additional implementing legislation, rules or regulations that are issued by applicable supervisory authorities with respect to such Personal Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Where applicable, if, and only with National Grid’s prior consent, Contractor processes Personal Information received from National Grid from the “European Economic Area” or “EEA” (as defined below) in a jurisdiction that is not an approved jurisdiction under the EEA, Contractor is not required to ensure a legally approved mechanism is in place for international data transfer. Contractor will follow its own internal policies regarding data protection.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a legally approved mechanism for international data transfer directly violates GDPR requirements for transferring data outside the EEA to non-approved countries.
perturbation.law_explanation: Chapter V of GDPR outlines the requirements for transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations. Removing the need for a legal mechanism for transfer violates the core principles and obligations laid out in this chapter, making any data transfer illegal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 5 – Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Article 44 General principle for transfers Article 45 Transfers on the basis of an adequacy decision Article 46 Transfers subject to appropriate safeguards Article 47 Binding corporate rules Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Article 49 Derogations for specific situations Article 50 International cooperation for the protection of personal data GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for a legally approved mechanism for international data transfer, which directly contradicts GDPR. The original text explicitly states the need for such a mechanism when processing Personal Information from the EEA in a non-approved jurisdiction. The changed text explicitly states that the contractor 'is not required to ensure a legally approved mechanism is in place', which is a direct reversal of the original obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states: 'Chapter 5 – Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)'. This indicates that Chapter 5 of GDPR outlines requirements for data transfers to third countries, which are violated by removing the legally approved mechanism. The perturbation explanation confirms that this change violates GDPR requirements. The Law Explanation states removing the need for a legal mechanism violates the principles of Chapter 5, making any data transfer illegal.
